entries.description: Python files should include SPDX headers as comments at the top of the file, before any code or docstrings
TypeScript files should include SPDX headers as line comments at the top of the file
JavaScript files should include SPDX headers as line comments at the top of the file
C source files should include SPDX headers as line comments or block comments at the top of the file
C++ source files should include SPDX headers as line comments at the top of the file
C and C++ header files should include SPDX headers as line comments, placed before include guards or pragma once
Java files should include SPDX headers as line comments at the top of the file, before the package declaration
Rust files should include SPDX headers as line comments at the top of the file
entries.example:
// SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2026 Your Name <email@example.com>
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT OR Apache-2.0

entries.description: Go files should include SPDX headers as line comments at the top of the file, before the package declaration
Shell scripts should include SPDX headers after the shebang line
YAML files should include SPDX headers as comments at the top of the file
YAML files (with .yml extension) follow the same header format as .yaml files
Markdown files should include SPDX headers as HTML comments at the top of the file
CSS files should include SPDX headers as block comments at the top of the file
HTML files should include SPDX headers as HTML comments at the top of the file, before the DOCTYPE declaration

The .reuse/dep5 file provides bulk licensing for files that cannot contain headers (binaries, data files, generated code). It uses the DEP-5 / machine-readable debian/copyright format. Each paragraph specifies a glob pattern and the associated copyright and license
entries.example:
Format: https://www.debian.org/doc/packaging-manuals/copyright-format/1.0/
Upstream-Name: My Project
Upstream-Contact: maintainer@example.com

Files: assets/*
Copyright: 2026 Your Name <email@example.com>
License: CC-BY-4.0

Files: data/*.json
Copyright: 2026 Your Name <email@example.com>
License: MIT

entries.description: SPDX license expressions support combining licenses with AND, OR, and WITH operators. AND means all licenses apply simultaneously. OR means the licensee can choose. WITH adds a license exception. Parentheses group sub-expressions
entries.example:
# Single license:
# SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT

# Dual license (user chooses):
# SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT OR Apache-2.0

# Both apply:
# SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0-only AND MIT

# License with exception:
# SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0-only WITH Classpath-exception-2.0

# Complex expression:
# SPDX-License-Identifier: (MIT OR Apache-2.0) AND BSD-3-Clause

entries.description: A project is REUSE-compliant when every file in the repository has a license and copyright associated with it — either via an SPDX header in the file itself, a .license companion file, or a .reuse/dep5 entry. The project must also include the full license text for every license used, stored in the LICENSES/ directory using SPDX identifiers as filenames (e.g., LICENSES/MIT.txt)
entries.example:
project/
  LICENSES/
    MIT.txt
    Apache-2.0.txt
  .reuse/
    dep5
  src/
    main.py          (has SPDX header)
  assets/
    logo.png          (covered by .reuse/dep5)
  README.md            (has SPDX HTML comment header)

entries.description: Binary files cannot contain SPDX headers. Use one of two approaches: (1) create a companion .license file next to the binary (e.g., logo.png.license), or (2) add a .reuse/dep5 entry that covers the binary via a glob pattern. The companion .license file approach is preferred for individual files; dep5 is better for entire directories of binaries
entries.example:
# Companion file approach:
# Create logo.png.license with:
SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2026 Your Name <email@example.com>
SPDX-License-Identifier: CC-BY-4.0

# dep5 approach:
Files: assets/*.png
Copyright: 2026 Your Name <email@example.com>
License: CC-BY-4.0

entries.description: Generated files (build outputs, lock files, minified bundles) should be covered by .reuse/dep5 rather than modifying the generation process to inject headers. If generated files are committed to the repository, they must be covered. If they are in .gitignore, they do not need coverage. Lock files (package-lock.json, yarn.lock) can be covered by dep5
entries.example:
# In .reuse/dep5:
Files: dist/*
Copyright: 2026 Your Name <email@example.com>
License: MIT

Files: package-lock.json
Copyright: 2026 Your Name <email@example.com>
License: MIT

entries.description: Full license texts must be stored in the LICENSES/ directory at the project root. Each file must be named using the SPDX identifier followed by .txt (e.g., LICENSES/MIT.txt, LICENSES/Apache-2.0.txt). License texts can be obtained from the SPDX license list or the reuse tool's download command

The SPDX-FileCopyrightText tag supports multiple formats: a year and name, a year range and name, or just a name. Multiple copyright holders are expressed as multiple SPDX-FileCopyrightText lines. The email address is optional but recommended
entries.example:
# Single holder:
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2026 Jane Smith <jane@example.com>

# Year range:
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2020-2026 Jane Smith

# Multiple holders:
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2024 Original Author
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2026 New Contributor <new@example.com>

entries.description: Any file can have a companion .license file placed next to it, containing the SPDX-FileCopyrightText and SPDX-License-Identifier tags. This is an alternative to in-file headers and .reuse/dep5. The companion file takes the original filename with .license appended (e.g., image.png.license)
entries.example:
# Contents of image.png.license:
SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2026 Your Name <email@example.com>
SPDX-License-Identifier: CC-BY-4.0
